name: Semaglutide
administration: Weekly subcutaneous injection (Ozempic/Wegovy) or daily oral tablet (Rybelsus)

dosing.weightLoss: 0.25mg weekly (weeks 1-4), titrate up to 2.4mg weekly over 16-20 weeks

compoundedNote: Compounded semaglutide available from 503A and 503B pharmacies at lower cost. FDA shortage status affects availability.
description: Semaglutide is the most widely prescribed GLP-1 medication for weight loss. Originally developed for type 2 diabetes as Ozempic, it was later approved at a higher dose as Wegovy specifically for chronic weight management. It works by mimicking the GLP-1 hormone, reducing appetite and slowing gastric emptying.

name: Tirzepatide

dosing.weightLoss: 2.5mg weekly (weeks 1-4), titrate up to 15mg weekly over 20+ weeks

compoundedNote: Compounded tirzepatide available from 503A and 503B pharmacies. Typically more expensive than compounded semaglutide.
description: Tirzepatide is a dual-action GIP/GLP-1 receptor agonist that has shown the highest weight loss results in clinical trials. It activates both the GIP and GLP-1 receptors, providing enhanced glucose control and appetite suppression compared to GLP-1-only medications.

name: Liraglutide

dosing.weightLoss: 0.6mg daily (week 1), titrate up to 3.0mg daily over 5 weeks

description: Liraglutide was one of the first GLP-1 medications approved for weight management (as Saxenda). It requires daily injections rather than weekly, which some patients find less convenient. While effective, it generally produces less weight loss than newer options like semaglutide and tirzepatide.

name: Dulaglutide

dosing.diabetes: 0.75mg weekly, may increase to 1.5mg, 3.0mg, or 4.5mg weekly

description: Dulaglutide (Trulicity) is approved for type 2 diabetes but not specifically for weight management. It can produce modest weight loss as a secondary benefit. It uses a pre-filled, easy-to-use pen that some patients prefer.

name: Exenatide
description: Exenatide was the first GLP-1 receptor agonist approved in the US. While effective for blood sugar control, it produces less weight loss than newer GLP-1 medications and is primarily used for type 2 diabetes management.

name: Survodutide
approvedFor: In clinical trials - not yet approved

dosing.trials: Titrated up to 4.8mg or 6.0mg weekly in phase 3 trials

averageWeightLoss: ~19% of body weight in phase 2 trials
description: Survodutide is an investigational dual glucagon/GLP-1 receptor agonist in late-stage clinical trials. It works differently from tirzepatide by combining glucagon and GLP-1 activity (rather than GIP and GLP-1), which may offer advantages for liver fat reduction.

name: Orforglipron
approvedFor: In clinical trials - not yet approved
averageWeightLoss: ~14.7% of body weight at 36 weeks (45mg dose, phase 2)
description: Orforglipron is an investigational oral GLP-1 receptor agonist that does not require injection. Unlike Rybelsus (oral semaglutide), it is a non-peptide molecule that can be taken without fasting restrictions and may be more affordable to manufacture.

name: Retatrutide
approvedFor: In clinical trials - not yet approved

dosing.trials: Titrated up to 12mg weekly in phase 2 trials

averageWeightLoss: ~24% of body weight at 48 weeks (12mg dose, phase 2)
description: Retatrutide is an investigational triple-action receptor agonist targeting GIP, GLP-1, and glucagon receptors simultaneously. Phase 2 trials showed the highest weight loss of any obesity medication tested, with participants losing up to 24% of body weight.
